speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two. Today's date is November 26, 1995-- interview with Clara Posner. Mrs. Posner, you were-- you were telling me some of your earliest memories when the war first started.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said the-- the Germans came in. Can you tell me what you saw? Can you tell me specifically what you saw them doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they came in-- I told you when the-- burned the houses. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- was it-- were-- was it at all the houses-- or which houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Streets. Maybe 1/2 a street, you know what I mean? Maybe 1/2 a street-- that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when that happened? Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I run away to my mother's house with my baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when you were-- when the war first started, where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a different house. Yeah. But when--

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the-- in the same town?

speaker: interviewee
text: When it started-- when it started-- the war, is among [NON-ENGLISH]. They start. She, you know, in-- right away, I took my baby. And we-- and I run away to my mother's, because there it was more quiet there. This-- this street where my mother was living was not so-- you know, it was quieter difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you had a baby. What was your baby's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moishe, like after my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a baby-- a year old, maybe. He was born 10 months after I got-- I got married. I got married-- I was only about 19 and 1/2 year old. He was born 10 month after my marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name was Moishe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So the Germans came in. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: In five days, when they came in, they-- I was going in, you know, that every man, from 16 to 60, they came. And the-- there was a big bizarre out, you know, a square one, you know. I don't know what you call this-- a [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know how to call this-- and to had to come there to register. Then everybody went--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --from 16 to 60.

speaker: interviewer
text: For-- for who? Just for--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what they wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who was supposed to register? Only men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they tell people this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people know they were supposed to register?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some was going in, you know, signs in the streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you remember seeing signs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And everybody went. And I said to mine older brother, don't go. We made a hiding place in our-- in our house. I said, go in the hiding place. Don't go. I'll go and I'll see what is gonna-- what's gonna happen there. I didn't let him go.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went. And there was maybe 500, 600 people. Who knows how much there was? And they came-- take a car. And five SS men came out with the guns like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they went-- took out one-- five people. They shot them right away. I saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? Do you remember when it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was five days after they came!

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- where was it? It was in Liuboml?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah!

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: In our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: --where, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how to call this. It was like where the store was-- a big, you know-- a square. Like, I don't know. How do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: A center.

speaker: interviewee
text: A center-- something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: A center.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what you saw--

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw they took out--

speaker: interviewer
text: --more specifically?

speaker: interviewee
text: --come out-- one-- five. I know the people. I-- I remember. A few, I remember. And they shot them right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then everybody start running.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who shot them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans who shot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know why?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Because we were Jews. You know why. That's the way they want it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what happened next? What did the-- what did the other people do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They run home. They run-- start running like crazy. What-- you know-- they was afraid, everybody, you know. And then I remember one month later-- exactly a month later, I remember. It was on a Wednesday. I-- I-- I remember. It was on a Wednesday. A month later, is a-- it came two big trucks covered with-- I don't know they call this-- big trucks. And they said they need 200 men to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they caught-- what-- what they men-- who they saw, they caught, they put in the trucks-- in the trucks. They took them away. And then when the night came, nobody came home. And all the mothers or the wives, they said, where are the-- where are the people? Nobody came home-- one day, the next day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then a Ukrainian lady came in our street, and she said-- she was a-- I think maybe she was a good lady. I don't know. She said, don't look for the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they made two graves. The people, themself-- the German told them to make grave-- two graves-- under to one and under to the others. And they did a few shots-- and that's all. And they covered the graves, because not everybody was-- a lot of them was alive, the lady said.

speaker: interviewee
text: They heard them crying and-- and they start, you know, they want to come out, but they couldn't. They covered already-- the German. And that's all. That was the first month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know. What do you mean, who told me? Everybody knows. At that time, they took mine-- mine uncle, my mother's sister's husband. At that time, they took him. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Noah, I remember-- mine uncle, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Noah. What was his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mandelsman. I remember, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, a month later-- just a month later-- the same day, Wednesday-- everything they did on Wednesday. They came in on Wednesday. You know, when used to come Wednesday, if it was a-- we was all afraid. It's Wednesday again. We was afraid. And they're-- they caught-- they took-- came in again with the bosses-- trucks covered like this, you know. And that time, they caught everybody whom they saw-- ladies, and older and younger girls-- about 500. And they took them away. It was a little village about four or five miles from the city. And they shot them down-- all of them. I know. The-- the Ukrainian people a lot came and told us. And they [NON-ENGLISH]. So they buried them there. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Ukrainian people told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of them came-- a lot. There was a lot of them who was not so bad. And they came. The ladies came and told us. Don't look for the people. They shot them all. Sure. And so every day, every day, they did-- every day. They came to the ghetto. They-- if they wanted, they see somebody-- shot-- just like that-- for nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came to which ghetto? In--

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- there was a ghetto. There were two ghettos. One ghetto was a-- in the-- the street what my mother lived. And the other ghetto was on a different street. There was only people would work by them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the Germans-- people what they work for them. That's-- that's I know. That's-- I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where were these ghettos? What street?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my-- in the street where my mother used to live there was one ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what street was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- a lot-- a-- a long time ago, before the Polish, or-- came, the Polish occupied. The street used to call [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember they used to call it [NON-ENGLISH] Street. That's the way they called this. But then, it was for my times, they-- they-- they changed the name from the street. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did they change it to?

speaker: interviewee
text: They changed. In Polish, they used to call it-- I'll tell you in English. In Polish, they use to call it [POLISH]. That's means, in-- in English, November 11. Our house, my mother's house, was 23, September 11th. In Polish-- in English, it means September 11. But in-- in Polish, it was [POLISH]. [POLISH] mean 11 in-- in Polish. September-- yeah-- that's the way they changed the street. Yeah, I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remember that ghetto and another ghetto. You remember there was another ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the other ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other ghetto was-- they called this [POLISH]. In Polish, [POLISH] Street. [POLISH] meant the train was coming on this street-- the trains, you know-- [POLISH]. In Polish, they used to call this [POLISH]. That's the way they called it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was another ghetto, but we couldn't go there. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was that ghetto for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that ghetto for? Who was it for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Jews. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Only Jews. Only Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family-- where was your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the ghetto what my mother lived-- over there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was there with you? You were there and you're husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: And sisters and the brother, yeah. My brothers worked. They-- everybody used to work-- never mind not working. My two brothers, the older ones, they worked by the Germans. They worked there. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: They came home every night to sleep, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you-- where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: By my mother, there with the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a house--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not only ma. You know, there was about 15, 16 people in a-- in the room. Because the other people whose-- but for burned-- for burned the house-- they didn't know where to go. They-- they put the-- the German put all the people in the-- in the-- in the same-- in the house of us in the ghetto-- 15, 20 people in a room. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did-- where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't ask.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in your room?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my house?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about 15 or 16. Who remembers? You sleep wherever we could, you know. We slept.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in the ghetto? From when to when?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- a year. The ghetto started, I think, in '41. It was after the Jewish holidays-- after all the Jewish holidays-- something like this, you know. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: --yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did you know to go there? Did somebody-- did somebody take you there, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean somebody take me?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: We've been there. We'd lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what did the-- what did the Germans do to make it a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called it a ghetto. They closed the street, and that's all. We couldn't go nowhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't able to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. If I-- I can leave, but they-- they would shot me when they catch me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they told you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew it. They told us right away. They told us the ghetto, and that's all. Here, you're gonna stay. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like? Was it-- you said they closed the streets.

speaker: interviewee
text: A street?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they put something? Did they put something?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The street was a kind of street. We could go only in the ghetto, to the neighbors, to, you know, but not-- and then farther there was the Ukrainian street. We couldn't go there. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do for-- for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We didn't get. They gave us, every day, a piece of bread for-- for a person-- a little piece, a little piece. It was-- I'll tell you that it wasn't bread-- 1/2 maybe it was dairy-- sand or something. Who knows what's-- it was like a rock, you know? But we didn't get. I remember my mother got a girlfriend. She was a Ukrainian lady. But they used to come. The-- the church-- I want to say the [POLISH] in Polish. The church was in the ghetto. Those was Ukrainians there, and they used to come every Sunday to church. They-- they go. They came to the ghetto. They-- they wouldn't got a chance, because the-- the-- the church was there. And my mother got that friend. She used to-- when she went a little for dressmaking, and the girl, the Ukrainian, she was there with her. And she used to bring my mother leaves from the beets-- not the beets, only the leaves. And my mother was-- washed the leaves, and cooked them, and chopped them. And she brought us, not flour, something with-- something what they-- she used to gave the pigs. She brought us this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother mixed the leaves with that flour. And she made bialys. That's was. You believe it? Believe me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] It was good. It was good. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you got a piece of bread. Who-- who would give you the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. It was in the ghetto. They made a-- a little store. And every morning, they bring the bread. And we-- we went. We stand in the line. And everybody had a name on it. We-- children and we are-- and they gave everybody a piece of bread. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Only the bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got this every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then we got a big backyard, I remember. My sister and I, we went. And we, you know, we digged and digged and we made a little garden, you know? And we planted cucumbers and tomatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the lady, the Ukrainian, gave us the-- the seeds or something-- what do you call it? And we planted tomatoes and cucumbers. And we got, you know, a little bit. That's all. And the ghetto was until 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew it that it-- it will come a time they will kill us-- all of us. We knew it. We knew it. But we didn't-- we didn't know when it would come. But I remember one night, I'd been sitting with my sister and look in the window. I don't know. I said to my sister, it smells something. Something not good is gonna happen. It happened this night-- came the Germans and the Ukrainian police. And they took everybody out of the houses and shot them. Took them-- no, no. They didn't shot them. They took them, and they put them-- they brought them to the big synagogue in the big shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- do you remember when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was the end of the Jewish holiday, in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] . They called it [NON-ENGLISH]. That's this night.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what exactly did they do? The Germans and the Ukrainians came, and they took who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody! They caught-- they took them away and they put them overnight in the Jewish-- in the big shul. And in the morning, they took them. They made two big, big graves, you know-- some [NON-ENGLISH]. Not they-- they-- they chased after the Jewish people that went to work, and they digged the graves-- two graves for the Jewish people. We knew. We knew it is gonna happen. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in hiding. We made a hiding place in our-- in our house. We made a double wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me more about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We made-- my that uncle-- this the uncle who did made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Berel. He made it. He was a contractor. And my mother's-- my grandfather-- my mother's father-- and her-- the two brother was contractor. They build a big houses. And he made the double wall-- the double wall. And in the need, we-- he made-- to-- to go in-- a little, you know, a little place, and with a spring inside. Nobody can go in-- out we can with the spring. I don't know that he made it something, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all my-- the brothers and-- went to there, in the-- and my husband-- in the hiding place-- and myself, and the baby, and my-- my mother, and my sister, we went in the-- how do you call it? It's in the [NON-ENGLISH]-- in a-- The attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: --attic, yeah. We been there for three days. And I made a little hole, you know. And I looked out and I saw everything. Every day they chased-- they were Jewish people-- to-- to there. They made it. This place was-- they used to call it [NON-ENGLISH]. They made there bricks, a special place where they made the bricks. There was-- there was this place where they made the two graves. And there was all the Jewish people that was killed there. I used to saw. Because we marked-- I made a little hole in the roof, you know, in the attic. And I looked out. I was there three days. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you see while you were in there? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more of what you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw through the little hole. I saw what they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Tell me more.

speaker: interviewee
text: --every day they chased the people to the-- to shot-- to shoot them. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you where-- where they made the two big graves-- in this place where they made-- this is the place they used to call it, in Polish, [POLISH]. This means where they made the bricks over there. Yeah. And, yes, and three days I was hiding with my mother. And then my baby start crying. I didn't got what to give him to eat-- nothing-- three days. And I jumped from the attic. I jumped down, because when I-- we went to the attic. My brother took us a table. And we-- from the table, you know-- and then he took away the table. Nobody-- to knows it was covered with something. But when I need food for my child, I jumped. There was not a table. And I jumped. I didn't care if I break a leg or nothing. I jumped, and I came to my-- to the hiding place, to the double wall where my brother was there. I thought maybe they got something-- water or cheese-- something. I said, I have nothing for my baby. And they said they haven't got nothing. And while I was there, I hear shouting on the-- maybe the baby was crying. Maybe they hear. And they came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukrainian-- Ukrainian. My sister was escaped, but not my mother and the babe. They took it away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you went downstairs to get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And while you were looking for food, you heard shouting?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hear shouting on the top, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was upstairs?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my mother, my sister, and the baby, because I went downstairs. My sister escaped. When they started shooting, she escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she escape? Where did she go?

speaker: interviewee
text: She run!

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do-- you know. You know? We didn't care. She-- she didn't-- she think maybe if she run they could shoot. But she run away. She hided in that second-- in a-- a different house. She run away. She hide. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where was your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: And at night she came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your husband and--

speaker: interviewee
text: There in the wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my brother!

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I told you, I was jumping down, asking my brother if they got something-- to give some drink for the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they hided in the wall-- in the double wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and the Ukrainians came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't They couldn't see. They couldn't recognize us. It was so good made. They-- they came. I suppose they came. But they couldn't. You couldn't-- there was so good made, and painted, and everything. They couldn't see this, no. And we been there for nine days-- no food, no nothing, and-- and-- and nothing to drink. And then my little brother, the youngest, he was, at that time, about 15 or 16 year old. He said, I'm going in the next door. Maybe there's some water or something. But he went to the next neighbor, and the neighbor what-- he got arthritis. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't laugh, it's true. And he made every night-- he made a bath. And he took a pail of water from the-- from the-- from the-- bath and came. We drink that water. And you know something? We took a pail, you know. And we did everything. Nine days we been there. And then one night-- What-- what did you-- what did you do for food? You found some food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing!

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing! We got the water. Do not ask me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one night, we hear it's thundering-- terrible! But wait-- where we was hiding-- by every house was a German and a Ukrainian across, you know? They know-- they knew that there is people what hiding in the houses. They waiting. They said maybe somebody will run out. They'll shoot.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my brother said-- the older brother said, this night, they wouldn't be here. It's terrible. It's raining. It thundering. And this-- this night, we escaped from the hiding, from the wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- they started shoot through the walls a week-- nine days-- a week after the-- the end of the Jewish holidays-- 1942. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first one was my sister. She-- she didn't-- she said, I'll go the first. If you hear shooting, she said, do not-- don't go. But she didn't walk. She crawled like a dog, you know? But she couldn't. If they-- if she would have walked, somebody would see her. But she walked like-- crawl like a dog.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was, in the backyard, mine brother got a-- a-- what do you call this? A-- not a garage, a-- a-- how you call this in English? Like there we used to hold the-- the horses, you know? Stable.

speaker: interviewee
text: A stable. Yeah, a stable. And she went there. And it was quiet. The next one, I went. And then my brother. We didn't run. We crawled, all of us. And it's-- then it was raining terrible. And we washed the faces, and we drinked a little water. And we start going to the woods, because my brother knew the village very close. Because he used to make the same business with my father-- father used to make. Then when he was older, he used to go to the same villages. He made business with the-- there was a Polish village. But they-- people, they-- those people wasn't Ukrainian. It was Polish people. And they was very good people. They was very-- what we say-- like Orthodox, you know-- very, very good people. When they came-- when we came, and my brother came, they gave a piece of bread and everything. Yeah. They told us-- they told us that in church, the priest said, if you were to give him food, don't give it. But don't shoot. Don't shot him, you know. Don't doing nothing to him. This is the way they was.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- they-- we used to go back, and they used-- no. They didn't give a bread. They was very afraid to gave it, because if somebody will catch us, they ask, who gave you bread? They was afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: But potatoes-- we can take potatoes. We took-- we steal potatoes. In the basement, they used to have the potatoes. They didn't shut away the basement. They knew what-- where we go every night, every-- to a second night, to steal potatoes. They knew it. But they didn't say nothing. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what town was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a village.

speaker: interviewer
text: A village. What village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oye. [LAUGHS] Wait, wait. I forget about it-- Ziemlica. And the woods was there around in the-- around the village. They used to call it the Ziemlica woods. They used to call it like this. And we went-- we went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you. We used go to steal food. That's all. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the ground. [LAUGHS] On the ground. And we were there together about three months. It was a week-- I think about five, six days before Christmas. And I remember it was terrible cold-- terrible cold. And we made a little fire just to warm a little bit. And it was-- the woods was about four or five miles from the village. And there was Germans. And there was Ukrainian-- did-- at you. And they maybe-- the-- they smelled the-- the-- the smoke. I don't know. And we didn't know nothing. And they came and start shooting. And they killed everybody. That's all-- everybody. Oh, OK. All right.
